title: Interaction-Phase Dynamics and Spectral Organization in Damped Higher-Order Nonlinear Schrödinger Models
abstract: We investigate the dynamical mechanisms underlying the contrasting nonlinear Floquet spectral evolutions observed in viscous and nonlinear mean-flow damped higher-order nonlinear Schr"odinger systems. Motivated by the persistent organized Floquet-band structure under nonlinear mean-flow damping and the repeated Floquet band reconnection observed under viscous damping, we derive a reduced five-mode carrier-sideband truncation and reformulate the dynamics in amplitude-phase variables to isolate the dominant interaction phases associated with the principal four-wave interaction products. Within this framework, viscous damping acts primarily modewise and does not directly modify the leading interaction-phase dynamics. By contrast, nonlinear mean-flow damping contributes directly to the interaction-phase evolution through interaction-dependent dissipative corrections. In the carrier-sideband regime, these corrections generate terms of the form $-κ_j \sin(ψ_j)$, introducing dissipative feedback into the dominant carrier-sideband interaction dynamics. To interpret the resulting interaction-phase evolution, we examine recurrent finite-gap NLS benchmark solutions whose modulation dynamics are independently understood. These benchmarks show that substantial interaction-phase evolution and localized restructuring may occur even within organized quasiperiodic dynamics possessing invariant Floquet spectral structure. Numerical diagnostics show that the nonlinear mean-flow damped system exhibits persistent recurrent carrier-sideband focusing dynamics together with organized Floquet evolution despite substantial interaction-phase restructuring, whereas the viscous system exhibits progressively diffuse modulation dynamics together with repeated Floquet reconfiguration and weakening persistence of the recurrent carrier-sideband interaction structure.
summary: This paper explains how different types of dissipation fundamentally alter the stability of nonlinear wave structures. It shows that while viscous damping destroys spectral organization, nonlinear mean-flow damping allows for complex phase restructuring without breaking the underlying coherent Floquet-band structure.
main_problem: The study investigates why two different dissipative mechanisms—viscous damping and nonlinear mean-flow damping—lead to fundamentally different long-time Floquet spectral evolutions and modulation dynamics in higher-order nonlinear Schrödinger (HONLS) systems.
main_result: The author demonstrates that nonlinear mean-flow damping maintains organized, recurrent Floquet-band structures through interaction-dependent dissipative feedback, whereas viscous damping causes spectral reconnection, diffusion, and the breakdown of coherent modulation.
method: The research employs a reduced five-mode Fourier truncation and an amplitude-phase reformulation to isolate interaction phases, alongside numerical simulations and comparisons with integrable NLS finite-gap benchmark solutions.
model_or_system: The system consists of Higher-Order Nonlinear Schrödinger (HONLS) models subject to two distinct dissipative extensions: Viscous-HONLS (V-HONLS) and Nonlinear Mean-Flow Damped-HONLS (NLD-HONLS).
key_observables: Nonlinear Floquet spectrum (band structure, critical points, and reconnection), interaction phases (psi_j), wave amplitude evolution, and peak wavenumber (k_peak).
important_parameters: Viscous damping strength (Gamma), nonlinear mean-flow damping parameter (beta), higher-order dispersive correction (epsilon), and wavenumber (mu).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a five-mode Fourier truncation and assumes a carrier-sideband hierarchy (r_0 >> r_plus/minus_1 >> r_plus/minus_2) for asymptotic reductions.
figures_summary: Figures show the evolution of Floquet spectra over time, comparison of surface amplitude and wavenumber dynamics, and the evolution of interaction phases correlated with rogue-wave events and spectral restructuring.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the HONLS models and Floquet diagnostic framework, moves to the derivation of a reduced five-mode interaction-phase model, presents numerical comparisons between viscous and mean-flow damping, and concludes with benchmark analyses using integrable NLS solutions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Early-time Floquet spectra for HONLS, V-HONLS, and NLD-HONLS at (a) t = 0, (b) t = 0.5, (c) t = 1.5, and (d) t = 3.5 illustrating the common early asymmetric band splitting and the formation of a localized two-band spectral conﬁguration at t = 3.5.
Figure 2: V-HONLS with Γ = .002: Later-stage Floquet spectra at (a) t = 54.9, (b ) t = 55, and (c) t = 55.1 showing spectral reconﬁguration and a critical-point crossing.
Figure 3: HONLS (Γ = 0) and V-HONLS evolutions for varying damping parameter Γ: Timeline using SPB initial data showing critical point transitions: blue dots for real, red ’×’ for complex.

Figure 4: NLD-HONLS with β = 0.1: Later-stage Floquet spectra at (a) t = 15, (b) t = 23, and (c) t = 47, exhibiting persistent organization of the dominant spectral bands, absence of critical-point crossings, and gradual band deformation and enlargement.

Figure 5: One-mode ﬁnite-gap NLS dynamics: (a) surface evolution, (b) dominant spectral mode kpeak, (c) interac- tion phases, exhibiting strongly recurrent modulation cycles with persistent carriersideband interaction structure and negligible spectral drift.
